title: Spotify — Audio Streaming — System Design
description: Design a music streaming service: stream audio with instant start across a huge catalog, manage personal libraries/playlists, and support offline listening. The themes are segmented audio delivery via CDN, the catalog/library split, offline download/sync, and play-event signals feeding recommendations and royalties.

nodes.label: Music Client
nodes.description: Streams audio, manages playlists, downloads for offline.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes playback, library, and search requests.

nodes.label: Streaming Service
nodes.description: Authorizes playback and issues signed segment URLs.

nodes.label: Audio CDN
nodes.description: Serves audio segments near listeners; the hot read path.

nodes.label: Library / Playlist Service
nodes.description: User playlists, saved tracks, and library state.

nodes.label: Library Store
nodes.description: Per-user playlists and saved library, sharded by user.

nodes.label: Ingestion / Transcode
nodes.description: Encodes uploaded masters into streaming bitrates + segments.

nodes.label: Play Event Log
nodes.description: Play/skip events for recommendations, royalties, and resume.

functional: Search and browse a large music catalog (tracks, albums, artists).
Stream a track with near-instant start and adaptive quality.
Create and manage playlists; save tracks to a library.
Personalized home and generated playlists from listening history.
nonFunctional: Low start latency (first audio in well under a second).
Read-heavy streaming served globally via CDN.
Durable audio storage; catalog/library metadata consistent.
Offline listening works without connectivity and re-syncs later.
Capture play events reliably (for recommendations and royalties).

capacity.label: Start latency
capacity.note: Prefetch first segment + small initial buffer.

api.description: Authorize playback and return the streaming manifest with signed segment URLs.
Add a track to a playlist.
Download a track/playlist for offline (encrypted local cache).
Report a play/skip event (for recs + royalties + resume).

dataModel.name: tracks (catalog)
dataModel.note: Authoritative track metadata + audio asset references.

dataModel.name: playlists
dataModel.note: User playlists; ordered track references, sharded by owner.

fields.name: ms_played
fields.type: int (counts as a stream past a threshold)

patterns.name: Segmented audio + CDN
patterns.what: Encode tracks into bitrates and small segments served from CDN for instant start and adaptive quality.
patterns.whenToUse: Streaming media with global read fanout.

patterns.name: Catalog/library split
patterns.what: Separate the shared immutable catalog from per-user mutable library/playlists.
patterns.whenToUse: Shared content + personal collections with different scaling.

patterns.name: Reference-by-id playlists
patterns.what: Playlists store track ids; audio lives once and is referenced by many.
patterns.whenToUse: Same track in millions of playlists.

patterns.name: Offline download + sync
patterns.what: Cache encrypted audio locally; reconcile library changes on reconnect.

patterns.name: Play-event stream
patterns.what: Stream play/skip events for recommendations, royalties, and resume.
patterns.whenToUse: Behavior that drives personalization and accounting.

patterns.name: Async ingestion pipeline
patterns.what: Encode uploaded masters into streaming assets off the serving path.
patterns.whenToUse: Adding content to the catalog without blocking playback.

steps.title: 1. Segmented Audio Delivery via CDN
description: Audio is encoded (during ingestion) into a few bitrates and split into small segments. Playback returns a manifest of segment URLs; the client fetches the first segment immediately and buffers a little, giving near-instant start, then adapts bitrate to the connection.

Serve segments from a CDN, not the origin store — that's how tens of millions of concurrent streams are handled, with audio cached near listeners. The streaming service only authorizes playback and hands out signed, expiring segment URLs.
Tracks are far smaller than video, so the pipeline is lighter — but the structure (encode -> segment -> CDN) mirrors any media-streaming design.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make a track start playing in under a second from a 100M-track catalog?

concepts.definition: Bitrate-encoded, segmented audio served from CDN edge with a manifest.
concepts.whyItMatters: Instant start, adaptive quality, and massive read fanout without touching the origin.
concepts.example: Prefetch segment 1 at 160kbps; switch to 320 if bandwidth allows.

whyNow: Playback is the headline experience; CDN-served segments are how streaming scales and start latency is met.

recap.newRisk: Finding and organizing tracks (catalog + personal library) is a separate concern.

steps.title: 2. Catalog vs Library Split
description: Two very different data shapes: the catalog is huge, shared, and mostly immutable (tracks/albums/artists); the library is per-user, small, and mutable (saved tracks, playlists). Keep them in separate services/stores so each scales on its own terms.

The catalog service powers search/browse over the shared metadata (read-heavy, cacheable). The library service owns per-user state, sharded by user. Audio assets are referenced from the catalog; neither service stores audio bytes.
This split mirrors the e-commerce catalog pattern: a shared read-optimized catalog plus per-user mutable state.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why separate the shared music catalog from each user's playlists/library?

concepts.definition: Shared immutable catalog separate from per-user mutable library state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Each scales differently — catalog is cacheable/read-heavy; library shards by user.
concepts.example: One 'Bohemian Rhapsody' track; millions of playlists reference its id.

whyNow: The catalog/library split organizes the metadata side and sets up playlists as references — the natural structure for a music app.

recap.after: Searchable shared catalog + per-user library, cleanly separated.
recap.newRisk: Playlists referencing tracks must be efficient and not duplicate audio/metadata.

steps.title: 3. Playlists and Library (Reference by ID)
description: Playlists are ordered lists of track ids — never copies of audio or full metadata. A track appears in millions of playlists but exists once; the playlist stores references and hydrates track metadata (and audio manifest) on read.

The library service handles add/remove/reorder with the playlist owner's shard, returning fast. Collaborative playlists add concurrent edits (last-write or simple ordering reconciliation), but most playlists are single-owner.
Reads hydrate playlist track ids against the catalog (cached) for display, then playback uses the per-track manifest from step 1.
steps.decisionPrompt: A track is in 10M playlists. How do you store playlists without duplicating the track?
whyNow: Reference-by-id is the efficient playlist model and reuses the same 'store once, reference by id' idea as feeds — a clean reuse.

concepts.definition: Store ids in the collection; hydrate full objects on read.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds storage and makes shared items (tracks) editable in one place.
concepts.example: Playlist = [t_9, t_42, ...]; metadata + audio hydrated at read time.

recap.after: Efficient reference-based playlists hydrated on read.
recap.newRisk: Users want music without connectivity — offline download and sync.

steps.title: 4. Offline Download and Sync
description: Offline listening is a defining feature. Downloading a playlist fetches its tracks' audio segments (a chosen bitrate) into an encrypted local cache, with license/DRM so content isn't freely extractable.

Library changes made offline (new playlist, saved track) buffer locally and reconcile on reconnect — the same offline-buffer-then-sync pattern as other apps; playlists are small reference lists so the merge is cheap.
Track availability can change (licensing), so downloads are validated periodically and re-checked on reconnect; expired/unavailable tracks are handled gracefully.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user downloads a playlist on wifi and listens on a plane. What's stored locally, and how does the library stay in sync?

concepts.definition: Encrypted local audio cache + buffered library edits synced on reconnect.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables connectivity-free listening while keeping the library consistent.
concepts.example: Download 24 tracks; add a playlist offline; both reconcile when back online.

whyNow: Offline is the audio-specific differentiator (smaller files make it practical); the download + sync model is the key design point.

recap.after: Encrypted offline cache + library sync on reconnect.
recap.newRisk: Plays must be captured (offline too) for recommendations and royalties.

steps.title: 5. Play Events: Recommendations and Royalties
description: Every play/skip is a signal. Clients emit play events (including how long a track was played) to an event stream — buffered offline and flushed on reconnect so offline plays still count.

These events feed two consumers: recommendations (personalized home, Discover-style playlists from listening history) and royalty accounting (a play past a threshold counts as a billable stream). The stream decouples capture from these heavy downstream computations.
Recommendations reuse the candidate-gen + ranking funnel; generated playlists are written back into the user's library. Resume-playback position also comes from these events.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why route play events through a stream instead of writing each play to the DB synchronously?
whyNow: Play-event capture is both the recommendation fuel and the royalty source of truth; the stream decouples it from serving and downstream compute.

concepts.definition: A durable stream of play/skip events consumed by recs and royalty accounting.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reliable behavior capture (incl. offline) feeding personalization and billing.
concepts.example: Play > 30s -> counts as a stream; history -> Discover Weekly.

recap.after: Durable play events feeding recs + royalties + resume.
recap.newRisk: Popular tracks/artists create read and event hotspots; ingestion must scale.

steps.title: 6. Ingestion, Hot Content, and Scale
description: New content enters via an async ingestion/transcode pipeline: a master is encoded into bitrates + segments, stored, and registered in the catalog — off the serving path, so adding music never affects playback.

Hot content (a viral track, a new release) gets disproportionate reads — already absorbed by the CDN (audio) and catalog cache (metadata). Play-event bursts buffer in the stream with scalable consumers.

Shard catalog and library by their access keys; replicate catalog reads and CDN globally. Audio is durable in object storage; catalog/library are the authoritative metadata; CDN and caches are rebuildable derived state.
steps.decisionPrompt: A new album drops and everyone plays it at once. What absorbs the spike?
whyNow: Closing on async ingestion + hot-content scaling shows the design adds content cleanly and survives release spikes.

recap.after: Async ingestion, CDN-absorbed hot reads, scalable event consumers.
recap.newRisk: Licensing-driven catalog changes and royalty accuracy — bounded by re-validation and the durable event log.

bottlenecks.mitigation: CDN for audio + catalog cache; prewarm the CDN for big drops.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Playback and library are unaffected; recs are slightly stale; events queue durably and catch up.
failureDrills.mitigation: Decoupled event stream; playback never depends on recs.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Audio Streaming
finalDesign.description: An async ingestion pipeline encodes masters into bitrate segments stored in object storage and registers tracks in the catalog. Playback authorizes and hands out signed CDN segment URLs for instant-start adaptive streaming. A catalog/library split keeps the shared, cacheable catalog separate from per-user playlists (stored as track-id references, hydrated on read); offline download caches encrypted audio locally with library edits reconciled on reconnect. Play/skip events stream (buffered offline) to recommendations and royalty accounting. Audio is CDN-served and durable; catalog/library shard by access key; caches and CDN are rebuildable derived state.

nonFunctional.how: CDN edge for audio; cached catalog.

say: Confirm streaming + library + offline + recs over a huge catalog.
Pin instant start and global read scale.
Note play events for recs and royalties.
Segmented audio served via CDN for instant start.
Tradeoffs: bitrate choices, offline DRM, royalty-count thresholds.
Mention collaborative playlists and social features as extensions.

expectations: May miss offline sync and play-event pipeline.
Reasons about start-latency/prefetch and CDN prewarm for drops.
Discusses royalty accuracy and event-stream scaling precisely.

followUps: How do you guarantee royalty counts are accurate from (possibly offline) play events?
How would you support collaborative playlists with concurrent edits?
How do you prewarm the CDN for a massive new release?
How do you handle a track becoming unavailable (licensing) while it's in offline caches?
How would you add social features (friends' activity, shared listening)?
